topic: Someone discovers their friend has been lying about their job
raw_output:
[story 1]

The lukewarm coffee tasted like ash in my mouth. I stared at Mark, watching him meticulously recount the details of his 'innovative' marketing campaign. His voice was smooth, practiced, and utterly devoid of the frantic energy he usually brought to any conversation. Every time he paused to emphasize a point, I felt a tightening in my chest, a slow burn starting low in my stomach. "So, you're really doing the direct sales thing now?" I managed, my tone deliberately flat. I leaned back in my chair, crossing my arms tightly over my chest, a subtle physical barrier rising between us. I watched his eyes flicker, a momentary hesitation before he smoothly transitioned to talking about quarterly projections. It was becoming tedious; the forced confidence felt brittle. I wished the conversation would just end.

[story 2]

I picked at a loose thread on the arm of the sofa, my gaze fixed on Sarah as she spoke about her 'exciting new role' at the city gallery. Her stories were too polished, too rehearsed. I could see the slight tremor in her hand as she gestured, a tiny giveaway that something wasn't quite aligning. A dull pressure built behind my eyes. I forced a small, tight smile, nodding along when she described the gallery opening. Internally, I cataloged the inconsistencies—the vague answers, the way she avoided specifics about her actual daily tasks. When she finally finished, I cleared my throat, the sound sharper than intended. "That sounds… busy," I said, the word hanging in the air, weighted with unspoken skepticism. I shifted my weight, wanting to physically move away from the suffocating atmosphere of her manufactured narrative.

[story 3]

"Right, so you're still at OmniCorp, right?" I asked, the question sounding far too casual. I watched David fidget with the cuff of his shirt, his usual easy demeanor replaced by a stiffness I hadn't seen before. He launched into an explanation about a major restructuring, but his words kept tripping over themselves. I stopped mid-sentence, letting a silence stretch between us, heavy and awkward. My jaw clenched almost involuntarily. I didn't look directly at him, instead focusing on the pattern of the wallpaper across the room. The sheer effort it took to maintain my composure while processing the obvious untruth was draining. I finally let out a slow breath, the air feeling thick and heavy in my lungs. "Okay," I finally said, my voice low and level, "I see."
